network.overall_strategy: The network follows the literature consensus that prebiotic chemistry robustly generates racemic alanine through multiple convergent routes centered on HCN, acetaldehyde, α-aminopropionitrile, and pyruvate, while L-alanine is not obtained by any single validated abiotic synthetic step. Accordingly, the graph is designed as a complete connected bipartite network in which nine chemically supported pathways first build a DL-alanine pool from hydrothermal and surface chemistries, and a tenth pathway represents a late biochemical/prebiotic, explicitly speculative terrestrial symmetry-breaking module consistent with the literature view that alanine homochirality emerged indirectly at network level rather than by direct abiotic enantioselective synthesis.

reactions.name: Hydrothermal CO2 reduction to formaldehyde

conditions.catalyst: Fe(Ni)S-like mineral surfaces including greigite/mackinawite analog setting
conditions.other: alkaline vent conditions, pH 9-11, H2-rich serpentinization setting

reactions.mechanism: Mineral-surface CO2 hydrogenation under alkaline hydrothermal vent conditions yields reduced C1 carbonyls that feed later surface aldehyde chemistry.
reactions.reasoning: Formaldehyde production from H2 and CO2 on Fe(Ni)S catalysts under alkaline hydrothermal vent conditions is supported experimentally and is used here as the hydrothermal-to-surface carbonyl bridge; greigite/mackinawite are included because the vent literature emphasizes FeS-family catalysts in such settings (Herschy et al., 2014; Lane and Martin, 2012).

reactions.name: Hydrothermal pyruvate synthesis from CO2 by Fe/Ni mineral chemistry

conditions.catalyst: Fe/Ni mineral or nanoparticle surfaces; iron-promoted proto-metabolic chemistry
conditions.other: anoxic, reduced hydrothermal/interfacial conditions

reactions.mechanism: Stepwise CO2 reduction and C-C bond formation on reduced Fe-bearing mineral surfaces generates the α-keto acid pyruvate as a proto-metabolic hub.
reactions.reasoning: Pyruvate is a documented prebiotic branch-point intermediate formed in Fe/Ni-mediated CO2 reduction and iron-promoted reverse-TCA-like chemistry; this reaction is intentionally represented at network level because the literature identifies pyruvate formation but not as a single canonical stoichiometric elementary step (Muchowska et al., 2019; Beyazay et al., 2023).

reactions.name: Classic Miller-Urey spark discharge generation of HCN and acetaldehyde

conditions.catalyst: none intrinsic to spark chemistry; mineral surfaces collect and concentrate products
conditions.other: reducing gas mixture with CH4, NH3, H2, H2O under electrical discharge for ~1 week

reactions.mechanism: Atmospheric discharge chemistry generates reactive nitriles and aldehydes in situ, which dissolve into aqueous reservoirs and feed Strecker chemistry.
reactions.reasoning: Miller's classic discharge experiment generated alanine precursors including HCN and acetaldehyde, with alanine later formed through Strecker chemistry in the aqueous phase. Mineral agents are specified here as surface concentrators/collectors to satisfy the network schema for surface settings, while the actual energetic driver is the discharge (Miller, 1953; Miller, 1955; Johnson et al., 2008).

reactions.name: Volcanic H2S spark discharge generation of HCN and acetaldehyde

conditions.catalyst: none intrinsic to spark chemistry; sulfur-rich volcanic setting
conditions.other: volcanic gas mixture with CH4, NH3, CO2, H2S and electrical discharge; H2S-enhanced amino acid precursor production

reactions.mechanism: Sulfur-bearing volcanic discharge chemistry increases the flux of nitriles and aldehydes that subsequently enter Strecker networks.
reactions.reasoning: H2S-containing Miller-type volcanic discharge experiments produced substantially enhanced alanine-forming inventories relative to the classic apparatus, making this a separate literature-grounded precursor route to the same HCN/acetaldehyde hub pair (Parker et al., 2011; Johnson et al., 2008).

reactions.name: Surface photochemical generation of acetaldehyde from formaldehyde-derived carbonyl chemistry

conditions.catalyst: UV-driven photoredox surface chemistry
conditions.other: UV-exposed cyanosulfidic/photoredox setting; aldehyde precursor network rather than single clean stoichiometric step

reactions.mechanism: Photoredox homologation and carbonyl-network chemistry convert reduced C1 feedstocks into higher aldehydes, including acetaldehyde, under UV-exposed surface conditions.
reactions.reasoning: The literature identifies photoredox/cyanosulfidic chemistry as a route to the aldehyde precursors needed for amino acid synthesis, including acetaldehyde-forming networks. This reaction is intentionally coarse-grained but tied to documented aldehyde-precursor generation rather than the invalid direct shortcuts criticized in earlier attempts (Ritson and Sutherland, 2013; Patel et al., 2015).

reactions.name: Direct Strecker formation of alpha-aminopropionitrile from acetaldehyde

conditions.catalyst: mineral concentration surfaces
conditions.other: mild aqueous conditions; acidic or basic pH; relatively concentrated NH3 and HCN

reactions.mechanism: Acetaldehyde condenses with ammonia to ethanimine, followed by cyanide addition to give α-aminopropionitrile.
reactions.reasoning: This is the canonical Strecker route to racemic alanine precursor α-aminopropionitrile from acetaldehyde, ammonia, and HCN. Ethanimine is included explicitly because the deep review identifies it as a mechanistic intermediate. Nitrogen isotope behavior of α-APN is also documented, supporting pathway realism (Miller, 1955; Miller and Van Trump, 1981; Chimiak et al., 2022).

reactions.name: Cyanosulfidic phosphoro-Strecker synthesis of alpha-aminopropionitrile

conditions.catalyst: Cu(I)-Cu(II) photoredox cycling in a sulfide/phosphate-rich setting
conditions.other: neutral pH, H2S, UV irradiation; reported ~70% aminonitrile yield from acetaldehyde

reactions.mechanism: UV-driven cyanosulfidic chemistry under phosphoro-Strecker conditions channels acetaldehyde into α-aminopropionitrile efficiently.
reactions.reasoning: The deep review specifically states that α-aminopropionitrile forms at about 70% yield from acetaldehyde under phosphoro-Strecker cyanosulfidic conditions at 35°C and neutral pH. This is therefore a distinct, highly supported surface route converging on the same aminonitrile hub (Patel et al., 2015; Ritson and Sutherland, 2013).

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of alpha-aminopropionitrile to alaninamide

conditions.catalyst: mineral-assisted aqueous hydrolysis
conditions.other: sequential nitrile hydrolysis under mild aqueous conditions

reactions.mechanism: The nitrile group of α-aminopropionitrile is hydrated to the corresponding amide, alaninamide.
reactions.reasoning: Sequential hydrolysis of α-aminopropionitrile through alaninamide to alanine is the established Strecker completion sequence discussed in the review; this reaction separates the steps explicitly to avoid overcompression (Strecker, 1850; Miller, 1955; Miller and Van Trump, 1981).

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of alaninamide to DL-alanine

conditions.catalyst: mineral-assisted aqueous hydrolysis
conditions.other: continued hydrolytic maturation of Strecker product

reactions.mechanism: The amide is hydrolyzed to the free amino acid, yielding racemic alanine.
reactions.reasoning: This is the final hydrolytic step of the Strecker sequence and yields racemic alanine, consistent with all literature findings summarized in the review (Miller, 1955; Miller and Van Trump, 1981).

reactions.name: Pyrite-coupled reductive amination of pyruvate to DL-alanine

conditions.catalyst: FeS/H2S to FeS2 redox-coupled mineral system
conditions.other: pyrite-forming conditions; reductive amination driven by oxidative pyrite formation

reactions.mechanism: Pyruvate undergoes reductive amination with ammonia, with reducing equivalents supplied by FeS/H2S-driven pyrite formation.
reactions.reasoning: Hafenbradl et al. experimentally demonstrated alanine formation from pyruvate by reductive amination coupled to oxidative pyrite formation, making this one of the strongest hydrothermal entries to racemic alanine (Hafenbradl et al., 1995; Wächtershäuser, 1988).

reactions.name: Mixed-valence iron mineral reductive amination of pyruvate

conditions.catalyst: mixed-valence Fe(II)/Fe(III) oxyhydroxide-like mineral system with optimal 1:1 Fe(II):Fe(III)
conditions.other: alkaline conditions near vent sediments, pH ~9

reactions.mechanism: Mixed-valence iron minerals mediate electron transfer for reductive amination of pyruvate to alanine in alkaline vent-like settings.
reactions.reasoning: Barge et al. showed pyruvate-to-alanine conversion driven by mixed-valence iron minerals, with a maximum yield at a 1:1 Fe(II):Fe(III) ratio under alkaline conditions relevant to hydrothermal vent sediments; magnetite/greigite are appropriate reduced iron mineral analogs within the required hydrothermal agent list (Barge et al., 2019).

reactions.name: Nickel-H2 reductive amination of pyruvate to DL-alanine

conditions.catalyst: Ni
conditions.other: pH 7-8; 30 mM pyruvate; mild prebiotic catalytic conditions

reactions.mechanism: Ni activates H2 to reduce the imine formed from pyruvate and ammonium, giving alanine under mild aqueous conditions.
reactions.reasoning: Kaur et al. demonstrated that nickel catalysts allow H2 gas at 5 bar to drive reductive amination of pyruvate to alanine at 22°C and pH 7-8. Because Ni rather than one of the required hydrothermal minerals is central here, the step is placed in the broader Biochemical/Prebiotic environment rather than forced into Hydrothermal classification (Kaur et al., 2024).

reactions.name: Bucherer-Bergs conversion of pyruvate to 5-methylhydantoin

conditions.catalyst: mineral concentration and carbonate/phosphate buffering surfaces
conditions.other: Bucherer-Bergs-type chemistry; cyanide/ammonia addition followed by CO2 capture; 80°C

reactions.mechanism: Pyruvate reacts with ammonia and cyanide to an aminonitrile-like intermediate, which captures CO2 to cyclize into 5-methylhydantoin.
reactions.reasoning: The recent hydantoin route highlighted by Pulletikurti et al. proceeds from pyruvate, cyanide, and ammonia through CO2 capture to 5-methylhydantoin, providing a modern literature-supported alternative to direct Strecker hydrolysis (Pulletikurti et al., 2022).

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of 5-methylhydantoin to N-carbamoyl alanine

conditions.catalyst: mineral-assisted hydrolysis
conditions.other: 80°C hydrolytic opening of hydantoin ring

reactions.mechanism: Hydrolytic ring opening of 5-methylhydantoin generates N-carbamoyl alanine.
reactions.reasoning: The Bucherer-Bergs alanine sequence specifically proceeds through 5-methylhydantoin and then N-carbamoyl alanine before alanine itself, so these intermediates are separated explicitly to maintain fidelity to the literature (Pulletikurti et al., 2022).

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of N-carbamoyl alanine to DL-alanine

conditions.catalyst: mineral-assisted hydrolysis
conditions.other: 80°C continued hydrolysis

reactions.mechanism: N-carbamoyl alanine hydrolyzes to the free amino acid alanine.
reactions.reasoning: This is the final documented hydrolysis step of the Bucherer-Bergs/hydantoin alanine route described in the literature review (Pulletikurti et al., 2022).

reactions.name: HCN oligomerization to DAMN under concentration conditions

conditions.catalyst: concentration by eutectic freezing or evaporation; mineral surfaces concentrate HCN
conditions.other: HCN concentration >0.01 M; eutectic freezing around 250K can concentrate HCN strongly

reactions.mechanism: HCN oligomerizes through dimeric and trimeric nitriles to diaminomaleonitrile, a key high-connectivity nitrile hub.
reactions.reasoning: DAMN is the canonical HCN tetramer and gateway to broader prebiotic nitrile chemistry. The review emphasizes that concentrated HCN in eutectic or evaporitic settings is needed for this branch, so the reaction is kept to DAMN formation only, avoiding the previously criticized overclaim of a clean direct DAMN-to-alanine step (Oró, 1961; Ferris et al., 1978; Miyakawa et al., 2002).

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of HCN oligomeric material yielding mixed amino acid pool including DL-alanine

conditions.catalyst: alkaline oligomerization followed by acid/base hydrolysis on mineral-rich surfaces
conditions.other: low-specificity product mixture; alanine is one component among many hydrolysis products

reactions.mechanism: Concentrated HCN oligomers/black polymeric material hydrolyze to a mixed suite of amino acids, including alanine.
reactions.reasoning: The literature supports alanine formation upon hydrolysis of HCN-derived polymeric material, but not as a selective direct DAMN-only transformation. This coarse-grained feeder reaction is therefore explicitly labeled as low-specificity and mixture-derived, correcting prior overcompression (Oró, 1961; Ferris et al., 1978).

reactions.name: Hydrothermal Fe/Ni-sulfide complex-mixture synthesis including alanine

conditions.catalyst: Fe/Ni sulfide mineral surfaces
conditions.other: 80-120°C, ~10 days; CO/KCN/CH3SH-type hydrothermal feed system; alanine detected among product mixture

reactions.mechanism: Hydrothermal sulfide-catalyzed carbonyl/nitrile chemistry generates a complex amino acid product spectrum that includes alanine.
reactions.reasoning: Huber and Wächtershäuser reported alanine among products from hydrothermal Fe/Ni sulfide chemistry. Because the literature support is for a product distribution rather than a selective mechanistic route, this reaction is explicitly represented as a complex-mixture source rather than a precise directed stoichiometric pathway (Huber and Wächtershäuser, 2006).

reactions.name: Photolysis of interstellar ice analog feed converted to surface HCN and acetaldehyde pool

conditions.catalyst: UV photolysis of H2O:CH3OH:NH3:HCN ice analogs followed by warming and release to surface pools
conditions.other: interstellar/cosmochemical precursor route feeding terrestrial surface pools after deposition

reactions.mechanism: UV processing of mixed ices generates nitrile and aldehyde precursors that, upon warming and aqueous reprocessing, feed terrestrial Strecker chemistry.
reactions.reasoning: UV photolysis of interstellar ice analogs is documented to yield amino acid-related organics including alanine after hydrolysis, and all necessary Strecker precursors are present in cosmochemical settings. Here the route is conservatively represented as supplying the terrestrial HCN/acetaldehyde precursor pool rather than claiming a direct clean alanine synthesis on Earth from the ice itself (Bernstein et al., 2002; Muñoz Caro et al., 2002).

reactions.name: Late prebiotic symmetry breaking from DL-alanine pool to L-alanine

conditions.catalyst: none established as a direct alanine-resolving catalyst
conditions.other: speculative network-level terrestrial symmetry breaking; likely indirect and amplification-based rather than a single elementary reaction

reactions.mechanism: A racemic alanine pool becomes enriched in the L-enantiomer through indirect systems-level symmetry breaking, potentially downstream of CISS-driven homochiral nucleotide chemistry, stereoselective polymerization, and amplification/crystallization effects.
reactions.reasoning: This step is explicitly speculative and included only because the requested target is L-alanine while the literature unequivocally indicates that all established abiotic alanine syntheses are racemic. Current evidence favors terrestrial, indirect, network-level symmetry breaking rather than a direct enantioselective alanine synthesis; candidate frameworks include magnetite/CISS effects, homochiral RNA emergence, and amplification in coupled reaction-crystallization networks (Ozturk and Sasselov, 2022; Ozturk et al., 2023; Ozturk et al., 2025; Blackmond et al., 2025).

pathways.name: Classic Miller-Strecker route
pathways.description: Electrical discharge in a reducing atmosphere generates HCN and acetaldehyde, which enter canonical Strecker chemistry via ethanimine and α-aminopropionitrile to DL-alanine.

pathways.name: Volcanic H2S Miller-Strecker route
pathways.description: H2S-rich volcanic discharge enhances precursor production before the same Strecker-hydrolysis sequence to DL-alanine.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal formaldehyde to surface aldehyde to Strecker route
pathways.description: Hydrothermal CO2 reduction yields formaldehyde, later converted through photochemical carbonyl chemistry to acetaldehyde that feeds Strecker synthesis.

pathways.name: Cyanosulfidic phosphoro-Strecker route from classic spark precursors
pathways.description: Classic spark chemistry supplies HCN and acetaldehyde, and phosphoro-Strecker cyanosulfidic chemistry converts them efficiently to α-aminopropionitrile before hydrolysis to DL-alanine.

pathways.name: Cyanosulfidic phosphoro-Strecker route from volcanic spark precursors
pathways.description: Volcanic H2S spark chemistry supplies the same hubs, followed by the high-yield cyanosulfidic α-aminopropionitrile branch and hydrolysis.

pathways.name: Pyrite-coupled hydrothermal reductive amination route
pathways.description: Hydrothermal mineral chemistry forms pyruvate, which is reductively aminated on FeS/pyrite-forming systems to DL-alanine.

pathways.name: Mixed-valence iron reductive amination route
pathways.description: Pyruvate generated by hydrothermal proto-metabolic chemistry is converted to DL-alanine by mixed-valence iron mineral catalysis under alkaline vent-like conditions.

pathways.name: Nickel-H2 mild prebiotic reductive amination route
pathways.description: Hydrothermal pyruvate feeds a mild Ni/H2 reductive amination step under near-neutral aqueous conditions to produce DL-alanine.

pathways.name: Bucherer-Bergs hydantoin route
pathways.description: Pyruvate reacts with ammonia and cyanide, captures CO2 to form 5-methylhydantoin, and hydrolyzes through N-carbamoyl alanine to DL-alanine.

pathways.name: HCN oligomer feeder plus late terrestrial symmetry breaking
pathways.description: HCN oligomerization produces DAMN and related polymers whose hydrolysis contributes DL-alanine to the shared alanine pool, after which L-alanine emerges only through a speculative late network-level symmetry-breaking module.
